title: Laundry Basket Dresser
summary: A plywood tower where three laundry baskets ride metal-angle rails like drawers — the internet-famous laundry room build, one strip of plywood per dresser.
description:
This is one of the most-copied laundry room builds ever posted, and the idea deserves its fame: a simple plywood box with lengths of 1-1/2" metal angle screwed to the sides as drawer runners, and standard rectangular laundry baskets as the drawers. Sorting happens at the machine — lights, darks, towels — and each 'drawer' carries straight to its bedroom.

The material spec is worth following: 3/4" plywood ripped into 15-1/2" strips (the plan calls them 1x16s), which one strip of a sheet yields per dresser. The metal angle intimidates people unnecessarily — the hardware store will often cut it, a hacksaw handles it in minutes, and pre-drilling three holes per rail is the whole trick. Wood-shy? The plan's own alternative is 2x2 wood cleats, glued and screwed.

Two details separate the good builds from the saggy ones. First, the installation tip hiding in step 3: screw each rail at a VERY slight downward angle toward the back, so baskets self-park backward instead of creeping out. Second, the safety line that isn't optional: if you stack two dressers, anchor the top one to a stud — a tower of laundry with a toddler pulling on baskets is a tip-over waiting for its moment. Measure YOUR baskets before cutting; the listed dimensions fit the common bushel style.

tools.note: Ripping the sheet into 15-1/2" strips — a guide keeps them straight.
Pre-drilling the metal angle — three holes per rail.
A hacksaw for the metal angle, unless the hardware store cuts it for you.
The source used pocket holes for the box; countersunk 2" screws work identically.
Marking rail positions — and setting that slight backward tilt on purpose.
Budget plywood needs filler and a coarse-then-medium pass before paint — the source's own regimen.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", ripped to 15-1/2" x 8 ft
materials.note: One sheet yields strips for two dressers — this line is one dresser's share.

materials.name: Metal angle, 1-1/2" x 1-1/2", 15-1/2" lengths
materials.note: Ask for it at the hardware store; they'll often cut it. 2x2 wood cleats substitute.

materials.name: Wood screws, #8 x 2", or 1-1/4" pocket hole screws
Wood screws, #8 x 3/4"
materials.note: Metal angle to the box sides.

materials.name: Wood filler, primer, and paint
materials.note: Semi-gloss survives a laundry room. 'Oops paint' is the traditional finish.

materials.name: Laundry baskets, rectangular
materials.note: Buy FIRST and identical — the box is sized to them. Not in the estimate.

cutList.note: The top overhangs — 26", not 24-1/2"; the plan flags it because everyone cuts it wrong.

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body: Rip the plywood strips, cut the parts, and join sides to top and bottom — pocket holes (source's method) or countersunk 2" screws, plus glue. Note the top is 26" against the bottom's 24-1/2": the top overhangs the sides by design.
materials: Wood screws, #8 x 2", or 1-1/4" pocket hole screws

steps.title: Square it with the back, then finish NOW
steps.body:
Rack the box until the diagonals match, then glue and screw the 1/4" back on with 1-1/4" screws — the back holds the square.

Then do what the source did: finish at this stage, before the rails. Fill every hole and plywood edge void, sand coarse then medium (budget plywood demands it), prime, and paint semi-gloss. Painting around installed metal rails is the avoidable chore this ordering avoids.

steps.title: Install the rails — tilted back on purpose
steps.body:
Cut the metal angle to length (hacksaw, or the store's cutter), pre-drill three holes per rail, and mark the rail heights off your ACTUAL baskets — the lip rides the rail, so space each pair one basket-height plus a hand's clearance apart.

Screw the rails to the sides with 3/4" screws at a very slight downward angle toward the back — the plan's best tip. Gravity then parks every basket rearward instead of letting it creep out at you.

Slide the baskets in. Stacking two towers? Anchor the top one to a wall stud — non-negotiable with kids in the house. Then go sort some laundry into the only dresser that empties itself.

images.alt: Painted plywood tower holding three laundry baskets as sliding drawers
